text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY NEW PATIENT CONSULTATION

PATIENT: Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo
AGE: 55 years
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: Dermatology / Surgical Oncology
INDICATION: Newly diagnosed Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma; BRAF V600E-mutant

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent who presents for new patient consultation regarding recently diagnosed Stage IV melanoma. She was referred by her dermatologic surgeon following excision of a primary left posterior shoulder lesion and subsequent workup confirming widespread metastatic disease.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reports that she first noticed a changing pigmented lesion on her left posterior shoulder approximately 6-8 months prior to presentation. She describes it as a mole that had been present for many years but became progressively darker, irregular in border, and began to bleed intermittently with minor friction from clothing. She brought this to the attention of her primary care physician, who referred her urgently to dermatology. Punch biopsy was performed, and pathology confirmed invasive superficial spreading melanoma. She subsequently underwent wide local excision of the primary lesion and a core needle biopsy of a right axillary lymph node that had been noted to be enlarged on examination.

Final pathology of the primary lesion showed invasive superficial spreading melanoma with Breslow thickness of 4.2 mm, Clark level V, ulceration present, 4 mitoses/mm2, and lymphovascular invasion. The right axillary lymph node biopsy demonstrated metastatic melanoma with the largest deposit measuring 18 mm and extranodal extension. Immunohistochemistry confirmed S100+, SOX10+, HMB-45+, Melan-A+, Ki-67 approximately 35%.

Molecular testing via a comprehensive 523-gene solid tumor NGS panel was performed on the lymph node biopsy specimen. This identified a BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and a PTEN p.R130Q mutation (VAF 31%). Additional alterations included TP53 p.R248W and RAC1 p.P29S among others. Tumor mutational burden was high at 42 mutations/Mb; microsatellite status was stable (MSS). PD-L1 TPS on companion diagnostic IHC was 45%.

For staging, she underwent whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated hypermetabolic bilateral axillary lymph nodes (right 2.1 cm, SUVmax 11.4; left 1.8 cm, SUVmax 9.7), multiple hypermetabolic subcutaneous nodules in the right flank (up to 1.4 cm, SUVmax 6.8) and left axillary region, and four FDG-avid hepatic lesions (largest 3.1 cm in the right lobe, SUVmax 14.2). No brain, bone, or pulmonary metastases were identified. LDH at time of staging was 387 U/L, approximately 1.5 times the upper limit of normal. This establishes M1c disease (Stage IV, AJCC 8th edition).

She presents today reporting a rapidly growing subcutaneous nodule on the right flank that has become tender and more prominent over the past 2-3 weeks. She has noted palpable lumps in both armpits for the past month. She endorses approximately 8 lbs of unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months, which she attributes partly to decreased appetite, and ongoing mild fatigue that limits prolonged exertion but does not impact her activities of daily living. She denies fever, night sweats, jaundice, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, hemoptysis, dyspnea at rest, or neurological symptoms.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Positive for unintentional weight loss (~8 lbs over 2 months), mild fatigue. Negative for fever, chills, night sweats.
Skin: Positive for healing surgical site on left posterior shoulder. Negative for new suspicious lesions by patient report.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Negative for headache, visual changes, hearing changes, or oral lesions.
Respiratory: Negative for dyspnea at rest, cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain.
Cardiovascular: Negative for palpitations, edema, or chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Mild decrease in appetite. Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, melena, or hematochezia. Denies abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: No complaints.
Musculoskeletal: Denies bone pain, arthralgias, or myalgias.
Neurological: Denies headache, dizziness, weakness, sensory changes, or gait disturbance.
Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction beyond what is managed medically.
Psychiatric: Denies depression or anxiety at this time; acknowledges fear and uncertainty about diagnosis.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Hypothyroidism -- on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; well-controlled
2. Malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type, left posterior shoulder -- newly diagnosed, as above
3. No prior history of autoimmune disease, inflammatory bowel disease, or colitis
4. No prior history of other malignancies

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

1. Wide local excision, left posterior shoulder melanoma -- recent
2. Core needle biopsy, right axillary lymph node -- recent
3. Appendectomy -- remote (childhood)

FAMILY HISTORY

Mother: Died of colon cancer at age 68; no known melanoma.
Father: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes; alive in his 80s.
Paternal aunt: Reportedly had "skin cancer," unclear if melanoma or basal cell/squamous cell.
Maternal grandmother: Breast cancer, diagnosed in her 60s.
No known familial melanoma syndromes; no known CDK4, CDKN2A, or BRCA1/2 germline alterations (germline BRCA1/2 wild-type on NGS report; germline CDK4 wild-type).

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo works as a landscape architect and has lifelong significant outdoor sun exposure, which she acknowledges as a likely contributing factor. She reports inconsistent sunscreen use throughout her career. Smoking history: 10 pack-years total; quit approximately 20 years ago. She is a social drinker (1-2 glasses of wine per week). Denies illicit drug use. She lives with her husband and has two adult children; strong social support system. She is fully employed and functional. She is of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent. Travel history non-contributory.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No known food allergies.

MEDICATIONS

1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (hypothyroidism)
2. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg as needed for minor pain
3. Vitamin D 1000 IU orally once daily
4. No aspirin, anticoagulants, NSAIDs, immunosuppressants, or corticosteroids

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing woman, alert and oriented x3, in no acute distress. Appears mildly fatigued but engaged and interactive. Mild temporal wasting noted.
Vital Signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temperature 37.1 degrees C, Weight 62 kg (down from reported baseline of ~65.5 kg).
Performance Status: ECOG 1.
Skin: Healing well-approximated linear surgical scar on left posterior shoulder, approximately 10 cm, without signs of infection or wound dehiscence. No other suspicious pigmented lesions identified on full skin survey. No jaundice, no palmar erythema.
Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. PERRL, EOMI. No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, mucous membranes moist. Neck: no cervical lymphadenopathy. No thyroid enlargement or nodularity appreciated.
Lymph Nodes: Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy present and firm on palpation -- right axilla with a firm, non-tender, poorly mobile node approximately 2.0 cm; left axilla with firm node approximately 1.5-2.0 cm. No inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Chest/Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or rales.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild right upper quadrant fullness on deep palpation; liver edge palpated approximately 2-3 cm below the right costal margin; non-tender to palpation. No splenomegaly. No ascites. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.
Skin of Extremities/Trunk: 1.5 cm firm, mobile, nontender subcutaneous nodule palpated in the right flank, with slight overlying skin discoloration; consistent with known subcutaneous metastasis.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation grossly intact. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  WBC: 7.2 x10^9/L (reference 4.5-11.0)
  Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL (mildly low, reference 12.0-16.0 for women)
  Hematocrit: 38%
  Platelets: 224 x10^9/L
  Differential: within normal limits, no blasts

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
  Total Bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL
  Alkaline Phosphatase: 98 U/L (upper limit of normal)
  ALT: 52 U/L (mildly elevated; ULN 40)
  AST: 48 U/L (mildly elevated; ULN 40)
  Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
  Total Protein: 7.2 g/dL
  Glucose: 94 mg/dL

LDH: 387 U/L (elevated; approximately 1.5x ULN -- prognostic significance in metastatic melanoma)

Thyroid function (drawn today): TSH 2.4 mIU/L (within normal limits on current levothyroxine 75 mcg)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal)

Note: Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 52, AST 48) noted in the setting of known hepatic metastases; hepatic reserve appears preserved at this time.

---

IMAGING SUMMARY

Recent PET-CT (whole body, 18F-FDG): As detailed above -- hypermetabolic bilateral axillary nodes, multiple subcutaneous nodules right flank and left axillary region, and four FDG-avid hepatic lesions (largest 3.1 cm right lobe). No brain, bone, or pulmonary metastases identified. Consistent with M1c disease.

No additional imaging performed today. MRI brain has not yet been obtained; will be ordered as baseline.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman presenting with newly diagnosed Stage IV (M1c) metastatic cutaneous melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder. She has multiple sites of active disease including bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, subcutaneous metastases, and hepatic metastases (four lesions, largest 3.1 cm). BRAF V600E mutation is confirmed on NGS. PD-L1 TPS is 45%. TMB is high (42 mut/Mb). LDH is elevated at 387 U/L, indicating high tumor burden and an adverse prognostic factor in this setting. ECOG PS is 1. She is a nonsmoker, has preserved end-organ function, and has no significant autoimmune history.

1. SYSTEMIC THERAPY -- INITIATION OF FIRST-LINE TARGETED THERAPY

Given the following considerations, first-line therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg orally twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg orally once daily (BRAFi/MEKi doublet) has been selected:

   a. BRAF V600E mutation confirmed: patient is eligible for BRAFi/MEKi targeted therapy.
   b. Rapid disease kinetics: the patient has a rapidly enlarging subcutaneous nodule, palpable bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, and four hepatic metastases, with LDH 1.5x ULN. The need for rapid tumor burden reduction favors starting with targeted therapy ahead of immunotherapy, as BRAFi/MEKi combinations typically achieve faster initial disease control.
   c. PTEN p.R130Q mutation identified on NGS: this alteration is associated with reduced sensitivity to MEK inhibition and possibly with PI3K/AKT pathway activation, and will be factored into surveillance for early progression and future treatment planning. It does not preclude initiation of this regimen but does inform prognosis.
   d. The COMBI-d and COMBI-v trial data support dabrafenib/trametinib as highly effective first-line therapy in BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma, with objective response rates exceeding 65-70% and median PFS of approximately 11-12 months.
   e. Immunotherapy (e.g., ipilimumab/nivolumab or pembrolizumab monotherapy) remains a viable second-line option and may offer durable benefit, particularly given the high TMB and PD-L1 expression. The sequencing question (targeted therapy first vs. immunotherapy first) was discussed with the patient; given disease kinetics and hepatic involvement, we favor targeted therapy as the initial approach, reserving checkpoint inhibitor therapy for subsequent lines.

   Prescriptions written today:
   - Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg orally twice daily (approximately 12 hours apart, taken on an empty stomach)
   - Trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg orally once daily (with or without food, same time daily)

   Patient education provided regarding:
   - Pill schedule and adherence
   - Common side effects: fever/pyrexia (most common, up to 70% with this combination), fatigue, nausea, rash, photosensitivity, hand-foot syndrome, hyperglycemia
   - When to call: any fever >38 degrees C requires same-day notification and likely dose interruption of dabrafenib; skin rash with blistering or mucous membrane involvement (DRESS/SJS precaution)
   - Strict sun protection required; patient's occupational sun exposure was discussed in detail, and she was counseled to wear full-coverage protective clothing and broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen
   - Drug-drug interactions: avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers, proton pump inhibitors may alter dabrafenib absorption
   - Contraception: although patient's menopausal status was not directly asked, will confirm; if premenopausal, teratogenicity counseling required

2. RESTAGING IMAGING
   MRI brain with gadolinium ordered today as baseline (not yet obtained from prior PET-CT, which showed no intracranial disease, but MRI is more sensitive). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast planned at approximately 8 weeks after starting therapy to assess early response.

3. LABS
   Baseline labs obtained today (CBC, CMP, LDH, TSH, LFTs as above). Will recheck CBC, CMP, LDH, and LFTs at 2 weeks and 4 weeks after therapy initiation, then per standard monitoring schedule.

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM
   TSH 2.4 mIU/L -- well controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Continue current dose. Will recheck TSH at follow-up.

5. FOLLOW-UP
   Patient to follow up in 2-3 weeks for toxicity check after initiating dabrafenib/trametinib. Urgent call-back instructions provided for fever. Restaging CT planned at 8 weeks.

6. MULTIDISCIPLINARY DISCUSSION
   Case will be presented at next Melanoma Tumor Board for multidisciplinary input. Radiation oncology will be involved if CNS disease develops. Surgical oncology is aware of case.

7. GOALS OF CARE
   Lengthy discussion held with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo and her husband today regarding diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment goals. She understands the diagnosis is Stage IV (metastatic), that it is not curable with current standard therapies, but that modern treatments including targeted therapy and immunotherapy have significantly changed the landscape for BRAF-mutant melanoma, with a subset of patients achieving prolonged disease control or durable responses. She expressed understanding and agreed to proceed with planned treatment. She identified her goals as maintaining quality of life and maximizing time with family, and she expressed willingness to tolerate treatment side effects to achieve disease control. She was given written educational materials and contact information for the oncology triage line.

   Advance directive discussion initiated; patient will discuss with family and follow up at next visit.

8. SUPPORTIVE CARE
   Referral to social work placed for psychosocial support. Nutritional counseling ordered given 8 lb weight loss. Palliative care introduction offered; patient declined at this time but is open to revisiting.

Time spent: approximately 75 minutes in total, more than 50% in counseling and coordination of care.

Signed: Attending Medical Oncologist, Melanoma/Cutaneous Oncology Program

Cancer status: present, with active metastatic disease, initiating first-line therapy.
